docs.title: Home
docs.text:
A collection of technical notes, guides, and quick-reference documentation for various IT and development topics.

📖 Table of Contents [Programming Languages](./Programming%20Languages) - [How C# Works](./Programming%20Languages/how_csharp_works.md) [Disk Management](./Disk%20Management) - [Compress Image CLI](./Disk%20Management/compress_image_cli.md) - [NCDU Guide](./Disk%20Management/ncdu.md) - [Ubuntu 25 Cleanup](./Disk%20Management/ubuntu_25_cleanup.md) [Docker](./Docker) - [Docker Cleanup Guide](./Docker/docker_cleanup_guide.md) - [Docker File Watching](./Docker/docker_file_watching.md) [Markdown](./Markdown) - [GitHub Emojis](./Markdown/github_emojis.md) - [Markdown Guide](./Markdown/markdown_guide.md) [Networking](./Networking) - [Docker Run in Production](./Networking/docker_run_in_prod.md) - [LXC Run in Production](./Networking/lxc_run_in_prod.md) - [SSH Setup Guide](./Networking/ssh_setup_guide.md) - [VPN Setup Guide](./Networking/vpn_setup_guide.md) 🚀 Features - **AI-Powered Note Generation**: Automatically converts GitHub issues into formatted markdown notes using Gemini AI. - **Automated Refinement**: Bot-driven updates to notes via PR comments. - **Easy Categorization**: Automatically organizes notes into logical directories. 🤝 Contributing We welcome new notes! This repository uses a **Gemini AI-powered bot** to help turn GitHub Issues into formatted technical notes. You don't even need to touch the code—just open an issue. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md) for full details on how to contribute and how the automation works. 📂 Project Structure 
.
├── Disk Management/      # Storage, cleanup, and image compression
├── Docker/               # Containerization and orchestration
├── Markdown/             # Guides and references for markdown
├── Networking/           # Connectivity, SSH, and VPN guides
├── scripts/              # Automation tools and AI-driven generators
└── .github/workflows/    # CI/CD pipelines for note generation and PR fixing
🤖 Automation & Tools This repository uses a **Gemini AI-powered bot** to streamline contributions. - **`gemini_note_generator.py`**: A Python script that uses Gemini AI (`gemini-3-flash-preview`) to transform raw issue text into clean, structured markdown. - **`gemini_pr_fixer.py`**: Handles iterative refinements based on PR comments. - **`issue_to_note.sh`**: A shell utility for basic issue-to-note conversion.

docs.title: Contributing to TechNotes
docs.text: We welcome new notes! This repository uses a Gemini AI-powered bot to help turn GitHub Issues into formatted technical notes. You don't even need to touch the code—just open an issue.

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Start for Contributors
docs.text:
1. Create a New Issue: Use the New Issue button.
  2. Format the Title: Use the format [Category] Title of your note.
    • Example: [Docker] How to clean up unused volumes
    • If you omit the category, Gemini will try to suggest a logical one.
  3. Write the Body: Provide the technical details, code blocks, or links in the issue description.
  4. Add the Label: Apply the contribution label to the issue.

docs.title: 🤖 How the Bot Works
docs.text: Once the contribution label is added: 1. AI Analysis: A GitHub Action sends the issue content to Gemini AI (gemini-3-flash-preview). 2. Formatting: Gemini cleans up the text, fixes typos, organizes headers with numbered sections, and adds contributor metadata at the bottom. 3. Pull Request: The bot creates a new branch (e.g., contribution/issue-123) and opens a Pull Request with the new .md file. 4. Notification: The bot comments on your issue with a link to the Pull Request for your review.

docs.title: 🔄 Refinement Loop
docs.text: If the generated note isn't quite right, you can refine it directly in the Pull Request: 1. Leave a Comment: Simply reply to the Pull Request with your feedback (e.g., "Please add a section on X" or "Fix the code example in the second paragraph"). 2. Bot Update: The bot will automatically pick up your comment, send it to Gemini along with the current content, and commit the updated version back to the Pull Request branch. 3. Reaction: The bot will add a 🚀 reaction to your comment when the update is complete.

docs.title: 🛠️ Repository Setup (For Maintainers)
docs.text: To ensure the bot functions correctly, the following must be configured in the GitHub repository:

docs.title: 1. Gemini API Key
docs.text: Add your Google AI Studio API key as a repository secret: * Go to Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions. * Click New repository secret. * Name: GEMINI_API_KEY. * Value: Your API Key.

docs.title: 2. Workflow Permissions
docs.text: Ensure GitHub Actions has permission to create Pull Requests: * Go to Settings > Actions > General. * Under Workflow permissions, select Read and write permissions. * Check Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests.

docs.title: 3. Labels
docs.text: Create a label named contribution (Case-sensitive) in the Issues > Labels section.

docs.title: 📂 File Structure
docs.text: Generated notes are placed in directories based on the category (e.g., Networking/, Linux/). If a category doesn't exist, the bot will create it automatically.

docs.title: The jpegoptim utility is the most direct way to hit a target file size on Ubuntu because it automatically calculates the necessary compression levels for you.
docs.text:
1. Installation If you don't have it yet, install it via the official repository:

sudo apt update && sudo apt install jpegoptim

  1. Basic Usage (Target Size) To force an image to be under 1MB (1000k), use the --size flag:
jpegoptim --size=1000k yourimage.jpg

  • Warning: This command overwrites the original file by default.

  • Advanced Options

  • Keep Original: To save the compressed version in a different folder (preserving the original), use the -d (directory) flag:

mkdir compressed_images
jpegoptim --size=1000k -d ./compressed_images yourimage.jpg
  • Strip Metadata: To shave off extra kilobytes by removing EXIF/GPS data:
jpegoptim --strip-all --size=1000k yourimage.jpg
  • Batch Process: To compress all JPGs in your current folder to under 1MB:
jpegoptim --size=1000k *.jpg

  1. Troubleshooting

  2. "Size goal too small": If jpegoptim can't reach 1MB even at its lowest quality setting, the physical dimensions (pixels) of your image are likely too large. You will need to resize the image (e.g., using ImageMagick) before compressing.

  3. Simulate first: Add the -n (dry run) flag to see the results without actually changing any files:
jpegoptim -n --size=1000k yourimage.jpg

docs.title: ncdu (NCurses Disk Usage) Guide
docs.text: ncdu is a disk usage analyzer with an ncurses interface. It is designed to find space hogs on a remote server where you don't have an entire graphical setup, but it is useful even on regular desktop systems.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
To scan the current directory:

ncdu

To scan a specific directory:

ncdu /path/to/directory

To scan the entire filesystem (starting at root):

ncdu /

docs.title: Useful Flags
docs.text:
• -x: Stay on the same filesystem (don't cross mount points).
  • -r: Read-only mode (prevents accidental deletion).
  • -o <filename>: Export the results to a file for later viewing.
  • -f <filename>: Import a previously exported result file.

docs.title: Navigation and Shortcuts
docs.text: Once ncdu is running, use these keys to navigate and interact:

docs.title: Movement
docs.text:
• Up/Down or j/k: Move the cursor.
  • Right or Enter: Open/descend into the selected directory.
  • Left or <: Go back to the parent directory.

docs.title: Sorting
docs.text:
• n: Sort by name (ascending/descending).
  • s: Sort by size (ascending/descending).
  • C: Sort by number of items.
  • M: Sort by last modified time (requires -e flag).

docs.title: Display Options
docs.text:
• g: Toggle between graph, percentage, or both.
  • a: Toggle between showing disk usage and apparent size.
  • e: Show/hide hidden files.
  • i: Show information about the selected item.

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
• d: Delete the selected file or directory (asks for confirmation).
  • q: Quit ncdu.

docs.title: Remote Usage
docs.text:
To scan a remote directory and view it locally:

ssh user@remote "ncdu -o- /path/to/dir" | ncdu -f-

This runs the scan on the remote server, pipes the output to your local machine, and displays it in your local ncdu instance.

docs.title: Ubuntu 25.04 Disk Cleanup Guide
docs.text: This guide covers methods to reclaim disk space by removing unnecessary packages, caches, and logs.

docs.title: 1. System Package Cleanup (APT)
docs.text:
Ubuntu uses apt for package management. Over time, cached installers and old dependencies accumulate.

  • Remove unnecessary dependencies: bash sudo apt autoremove --purge
  • Clear the local repository of retrieved package files: bash sudo apt clean
  • Check how much space APT is using: bash du -sh /var/cache/apt/archives

docs.title: 2. Snap Package Management
docs.text:
Snaps often keep multiple versions of the same application, consuming significant space.

  • List snaps and their versions: bash snap list --all
  • Reduce the number of old versions kept (default is 3, minimum is 2): bash sudo snap set system refresh.retain=2
  • Manually remove "disabled" snaps: bash # This script removes old versions of snaps set -eu snap list --all | awk '/disabled/{print $1, $3}' | while read snapname revision; do sudo snap remove "$snapname" --revision="$revision" done

docs.title: 3. Log File Management
docs.text:
System logs can grow indefinitely if not managed.

  • Check journal log size: bash journalctl --disk-usage
  • Vacuum logs older than 3 days: bash sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=3d
  • Limit logs to a specific size (e.g., 500MB): bash sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=500M

docs.title: 4. User Cache and Temp Files
docs.text:
• Clear Thumbnail Cache: bash rm -rf ~/.cache/thumbnails/*
  • Find large files/directories: Install ncdu for an interactive disk usage analyzer: bash sudo apt install ncdu ncdu /

docs.title: Automation
docs.text: The best way to automate this is via a custom shell script triggered by a systemd timer or a cron job.

docs.title: Recommended Automation Script (/usr/local/bin/sys-cleanup.sh)
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# Perform system maintenance
apt-get autoremove -y
apt-get clean
journalctl --vacuum-time=7d
# Clean old snap versions
snap list --all | awk '/disabled/{print $1, $3}' | while read snapname revision; do
    snap remove "$snapname" --revision="$revision"
done

docs.title: Scheduling with Cron
docs.text: To run this weekly, add it to root's crontab: 1. sudo crontab -e 2. Add: 0 0 * * 0 /usr/local/bin/sys-cleanup.sh

docs.title: GUI Option: BleachBit
docs.text: For an automated GUI experience, BleachBit is the industry standard. - Install: sudo apt install bleachbit - Automation: It supports a CLI mode (bleachbit --clean system.apt_cache) which can also be scripted.

docs.title: Docker Disk Space Management Guide
docs.text: Docker can quickly consume significant disk space with old images, stopped containers, and unused volumes. This guide covers how to clean up and manage Docker's footprint.

docs.title: 1. Quick Cleanup (The "Nuclear" Option)
docs.text: The easiest way to reclaim space is the system prune command.

docs.title: Basic Prune
docs.text:
Removes dangling images, stopped containers, unused networks, and build cache.

docker system prune

docs.title: Thorough Prune
docs.text:
Removes all unused images (not just dangling ones) and all unused volumes. Use with caution.

docker system prune -a --volumes

docs.title: 2. Targeted Cleanup
docs.text:
If you want to keep certain types of data, use specific prune commands:

  • Images: docker image prune (add -a for all unused images).
  • Containers: docker container prune (removes all stopped containers).
  • Volumes: docker volume prune (removes all unattached volumes).
  • Build Cache: docker builder prune.

docs.title: 3. Managing Log Sizes
docs.text:
Docker container logs can grow indefinitely if not capped. You can limit them globally by editing (or creating) /etc/docker/daemon.json:

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "3"
  }
}

Note: This requires a Docker service restart: sudo systemctl restart docker.

docs.title: 4. Monitoring Usage
docs.text:
To see exactly what is taking up space:

docker system df

For more detail:

docker system df -v

docs.title: 5. Automated Maintenance
docs.text: Consider adding a cron job to run a basic prune weekly: 1. Run crontab -e. 2. Add this line: bash 0 0 * * 0 /usr/bin/docker system prune -f (This runs every Sunday at midnight).

docs.title: Implementing File Watching in Docker
docs.text: This guide covers setting up hot-reloading/file-watching for a stack consisting of React, Kotlin Micronaut, and Postgres.

docs.title: 1. React (Frontend)
docs.text: React (via Vite or Webpack) usually requires CHOKIDAR_USEPOLLING=true when running inside Docker on certain filesystems (like Windows/macOS hosts) to detect changes reliably.

docs.title: Dockerfile snippet
docs.text:
# Use a development node image
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install
COPY . .
ENV CHOKIDAR_USEPOLLING=true
CMD ["npm", "run", "dev"]

docs.title: docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
frontend:
  build: ./frontend
  volumes:
    - ./frontend:/app
    - /app/node_modules
  ports:
    - "5173:5173"
  environment:
    - CHOKIDAR_USEPOLLING=true

docs.title: 2. Kotlin Micronaut (Backend)
docs.text: Micronaut supports "Continuous Execution" using the Gradle or Maven watchers.

docs.title: docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
Map the source code and use the -t (continuous) flag for Gradle.

backend:
  build: ./backend
  volumes:
    - ./backend:/home/app
  ports:
    - "8080:8080"
  command: ./gradlew -t run

Note: For Micronaut, ensure micronaut.io.watch.enabled=true is set in application.yml for development.

docs.title: 3. Postgres (Database)
docs.text: While Postgres doesn't "watch" files for code changes, you often want to watch initialization scripts or configuration.

docs.title: docker-compose.yml
docs.text:
db:
  image: postgres:15-alpine
  volumes:
    - ./postgres/init:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
    - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
  environment:
    - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password
    - POSTGRES_DB=notes_db

volumes:
  postgres_data:

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• Inotify Limits: If watching many files, you might need to increase the host's inotify limit: sudo sysctl -w fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288
  • Permissions: Ensure the user inside the container has write access to the mounted volumes if the app generates files (like build artifacts).

docs.title: Ubuntu Production Deployment Guide (Docker)
docs.text: Environment: Ubuntu 22.04/24.04 LTS | Proxmox VM | Residential ISP Stack: React (Vite) + Kotlin Micronaut + PostgreSQL

docs.title: 1. Ubuntu Host Preparation
docs.text:
Install Docker Engine on Ubuntu (Standard Repository):

sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y docker.io docker-compose-v2
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
# Logout and login for group changes to take effect

docs.title: 2. Connectivity: Path A vs Path B
Path A: Public IP (Direct)
docs.text: Use this if you have a public IP and can port forward 80/443. - DNS: A Record → Your Public IP. - Reverse Proxy: Use Nginx Proxy Manager (NPM).

docs.title: Path B: Cloudflare Tunnel (Recommended)
docs.text: No open ports required. Best for CGNAT or security-conscious setups. 1. Create a Tunnel in Cloudflare Zero Trust. 2. Copy your TUNNEL_TOKEN.

docs.title: 3. Production Docker Compose (docker-compose.yml)
docs.text:
This configuration uses a secure internal network and handles the full stack.

services:
  # --- Connectivity Layer ---
  tunnel:
    image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: tunnel --no-autoupdate run --token ${TUNNEL_TOKEN}
    networks:
      - internal

  # --- Frontend: React (Nginx) ---
  frontend:
    build: 
      context: ./frontend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.prod
    restart: unless-stopped
    networks:
      - internal

  # --- Backend: Kotlin Micronaut (JVM) ---
  backend:
    build: ./backend
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      - MICRONAUT_ENVIRONMENTS=prod
      - DATASOURCES_DEFAULT_URL=jdbc:postgresql://db:5432/myapp
      - DATASOURCES_DEFAULT_USERNAME=user
      - DATASOURCES_DEFAULT_PASSWORD_FILE=/run/secrets/db_password
      - JAVA_OPTS=-Xmx512m -Xms256m
    secrets:
      - db_password
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - internal

  # --- Database: PostgreSQL ---
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_DB=myapp
      - POSTGRES_USER=user
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD_FILE=/run/secrets/db_password
    secrets:
      - db_password
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U user -d myapp"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - internal

networks:
  internal:

volumes:
  pgdata:

secrets:
  db_password:
    file: ./secrets/db_password.txt

docs.title: React (Frontend)
docs.text:
Use a multi-stage Dockerfile.prod to serve static files efficiently:

# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN npm ci && npm run build

# Stage 2: Serve
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 80

docs.title: Micronaut (Backend)
docs.text: In your application-prod.yml, ensure you are using connection pooling (Hikari) and proper logging levels.

docs.title: 5. Summary Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Ubuntu Firewall: sudo ufw allow ssh and sudo ufw enable. (No 80/443 needed if using Tunnel).
  • [ ] Secrets: Ensure ./secrets/db_password.txt is not committed to Git.
  • [ ] Backups: Use docker exec db pg_dumpall for scheduled backups.

docs.title: Ubuntu Production Deployment Guide (LXC on Proxmox)
docs.text:
Environment: Ubuntu 24.04 LXC | Residential ISP

Stack: React + Kotlin Micronaut + PostgreSQL

docs.title: 1. LXC Provisioning (Proxmox Host)
docs.text:
Run these on your Proxmox Host to create the containers using Ubuntu 24.04:

# Update templates and download Ubuntu 24.04
pveam update
pveam download local ubuntu-24.04-standard_24.04-1_amd64.tar.zst

# Database (2GB RAM, 16GB Disk)
pct create 200 local:vztmpl/ubuntu-24.04-standard_24.04-1_amd64.tar.zst --hostname pg-db --memory 2048 --net0 name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,ip=dhcp --rootfs local:16 --unprivileged 1
# Backend (2GB RAM, 10GB Disk)
pct create 201 local:vztmpl/ubuntu-24.04-standard_24.04-1_amd64.tar.zst --hostname kt-backend --memory 2048 --net0 name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,ip=dhcp --rootfs local:10 --unprivileged 1
# Frontend/Nginx (1GB RAM, 8GB Disk)
pct create 202 local:vztmpl/ubuntu-24.04-standard_24.04-1_amd64.tar.zst --hostname react-frontend --memory 1024 --net0 name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,ip=dhcp --rootfs local:8 --unprivileged 1
# Cloudflare Tunnel (512MB RAM, 2GB Disk)
pct create 203 local:vztmpl/ubuntu-24.04-standard_24.04-1_amd64.tar.zst --hostname cf-tunnel --memory 512 --net0 name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,ip=dhcp --rootfs local:2 --unprivileged 1

# Start all
pct start 200 && pct start 201 && pct start 202 && pct start 203

docs.title: Database (LXC 200 - Ubuntu)
docs.text:
pct enter 200
apt update && apt install -y postgresql
# Allow connections from Backend (LXC 201)
echo "listen_addresses = '*'" >> /etc/postgresql/16/main/postgresql.conf
echo "host all all 192.168.1.0/24 md5" >> /etc/postgresql/16/main/pg_hba.conf
systemctl restart postgresql

docs.title: Backend: Kotlin Micronaut (LXC 201 - Ubuntu)
docs.text:
pct enter 201
# Install Java 17/21
apt update && apt install -y openjdk-21-jre-headless
# Deploy Micronaut JAR
# (Copy your .jar file via 'pct push 201 myapp.jar /opt/myapp.jar')
# Run as systemd service

docs.title: Frontend: React + Nginx (LXC 202 - Ubuntu)
docs.text:
pct enter 202
apt update && apt install -y nginx
# Copy build artifacts to /var/www/html
# Update /etc/nginx/sites-available/default to handle React routing and proxy /api

location / {
    try_files $uri /index.html;
}

location /api/ {
    proxy_pass http://192.168.1.201:8080/; # Point to Backend LXC
}

docs.title: Cloudflare Tunnel (LXC 203 - Ubuntu)
docs.text:
pct enter 203
apt update && apt install -y curl
curl -L --output cloudflared.deb https://github.com/cloudflare/cloudflared/releases/latest/download/cloudflared-linux-amd64.deb
dpkg -i cloudflared.deb
# Authenticate and run:
cloudflared tunnel run --token YOUR_TOKEN

docs.title: 3. Connectivity Matrix
docs.text: Service Hostname Internal IP External Route PostgreSQL pg-db 192.168.1.200 None (Internal Only) Micronaut kt-backend 192.168.1.201 None (Proxied by Nginx) React react-frontend 192.168.1.202 Proxied by Cloudflare CF Tunnel cf-tunnel 192.168.1.203 Outbound Gateway

docs.title: 4. Production Hardening
docs.text:
• Static IPs: Assign static IPs to LXCs in Proxmox Dashboard for reliable communication between services.
  • Fail2Ban: Install on the Nginx LXC.
  • Backups: Use Proxmox Snapshots and pg_dump to an external NFS share.

docs.title: SSH Server Setup and Connection Guide
docs.text: This guide covers the basic steps to install an SSH server, configure it for security, and connect to it from a client.

docs.title: 1. Server-Side Installation
docs.text:
On Ubuntu/Debian-based systems:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install openssh-server

docs.title: Verify Status
docs.text: sudo systemctl status ssh

docs.title: 2. Basic Configuration
docs.text: The main configuration file is located at /etc/ssh/sshd_config.

docs.title: Common Security Hardening
docs.text:
Before making changes, back up the original file:

sudo cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak

Edit the file:

sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config

Recommended changes: - Change Default Port: Port 2222 (Reduces automated script attacks) - Disable Root Login: PermitRootLogin no - Key-Based Auth Only: PasswordAuthentication no

docs.title: Apply Changes
docs.text: sudo systemctl restart ssh

docs.title: 3. Firewall Configuration (UFW)
docs.text:
If using UFW, allow the SSH port:

sudo ufw allow ssh
# OR if you changed the port:
sudo ufw allow 2222/tcp

docs.title: Basic Password Connection
docs.text: ssh username@server_ip_address

docs.title: Connection with Custom Port
docs.text: ssh -p 2222 username@server_ip_address

docs.title: Generate Keys on Client
docs.text: ssh-keygen -t ed25519

docs.title: Copy Key to Server
docs.text: ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub username@server_ip_address

docs.title: Execute a Remote Command (Without Logging In)
docs.text: ssh username@server_ip_address "df -h"

docs.title: Copy Files (SCP)
docs.text:
• Local to Server: bash scp local_file.txt username@server_ip_address:/home/username/
  • Server to Local: bash scp username@server_ip_address:/path/to/remote_file.txt /local/path/
  • Directory (Recursive): bash scp -r local_dir username@server_ip_address:/remote/path/

docs.title: SSH Config File (~/.ssh/config)
docs.text:
Avoid typing the full IP and port every time by creating an alias on your client machine:

nano ~/.ssh/config

Add an entry:

Host myserver
    HostName 192.168.1.50
    User colton
    Port 2222
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

Now you can connect simply with: ssh myserver

docs.title: SSH Port Forwarding (SSH Tunnel)
docs.text:
Expose a remote service (like a Web UI on port 8080) to your local machine:

ssh -L 9000:localhost:8080 username@server_ip_address

Now you can visit http://localhost:9000 in your browser to access the remote service.

docs.title: List Active SSH Sessions (On Server)
docs.text:
who
# OR
w

docs.title: Terminate All Other SSH Sessions
docs.text: sudo pkill -u username -t pts/1

docs.title: VPN Setup and Connection Guide (WireGuard)
docs.text: WireGuard is a modern, high-performance VPN that is easier to configure than OpenVPN or IPsec. This guide focuses on WireGuard for its simplicity and speed.

docs.title: 1. Deployment via Docker (Recommended)
docs.text: Using wg-easy is the simplest way to manage WireGuard via a Web UI.

docs.title: Docker Compose Configuration
docs.text:
Create a docker-compose.yml file:

services:
  wg-easy:
    environment:
      # Change this to your WAN IP or Dynamic DNS hostname
      - WG_HOST=vpn.example.com
      # Optional: Password for the Web UI
      - PASSWORD=your_secure_password
    image: ghcr.io/wg-easy/wg-easy
    container_name: wg-easy
    volumes:
      - ./.etc-wireguard:/etc/wireguard
    ports:
      - "51820:51820/udp"
      - "51821:51821/tcp"
    restart: unless-stopped
    cap_add:
      - NET_ADMIN
      - SYS_MODULE
    sysctls:
      - net.ipv4.conf.all.src_valid_mark=1
      - net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

docs.title: Start the Container
docs.text:
docker compose up -d

Access the UI at http://your-server-ip:51821 to create client profiles and download .conf files or scan QR codes.

docs.title: 2. Deployment on LXC (Proxmox/Standard)
docs.text: For a lightweight standalone instance, LXCs are ideal.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text: On the Host (if using Proxmox), ensure the WireGuard module is available and the container has nesting and keyctl enabled.

docs.title: Inside the LXC
docs.text:
1. Install WireGuard: bash sudo apt update && sudo apt install wireguard -y

  2. Generate Keys: bash umask 077 wg genkey | tee privatekey | wg pubkey > publickey

  3. Configure the Interface (/etc/wireguard/wg0.conf): ```ini [Interface] PrivateKey = Address = 10.0.0.1/24 ListenPort = 51820 PostUp = iptables -A FORWARD -i %i -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE PostDown = iptables -D FORWARD -i %i -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE

    [Peer] PublicKey = AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.2/32 ```

    1. Enable IP Forwarding: Edit /etc/sysctl.conf and uncomment: net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 Then apply: sudo sysctl -p

    2. Start WireGuard: bash sudo systemctl enable --now wg-quick@wg0

docs.title: Mobile (Android/iOS)
docs.text:
1. Download the WireGuard app.
  2. If using wg-easy, scan the QR code generated in the Web UI.
  3. Toggle "On".

docs.title: Desktop (Windows/macOS/Linux)
docs.text:
1. Install the WireGuard client.
  2. Import the .conf file provided by your server.
  3. Click "Activate".

docs.title: Port Forwarding
docs.text: You must forward UDP port 51820 on your router to the internal IP of your Docker host or LXC.

docs.title: Split Tunneling vs. Full Tunnel
docs.text:
• Full Tunnel: Set AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0 in the client config. All traffic goes through the VPN.
  • Split Tunnel: Set AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.0/24, 192.168.1.0/24. Only traffic destined for your home network goes through the VPN; internet browsing stays local.

docs.title: Kill Switch
docs.text: WireGuard clients often have a "Block untunneled traffic" option. Enable this to ensure no data leaks if the VPN connection drops.

docs.title: Dynamic DNS (DDNS)
docs.text: If your home IP changes, use a service like DuckDNS or Cloudflare with a DDNS script so your WG_HOST always points to the correct location.

docs.title: How C# Works
docs.text: This note aims to explain the fundamental workings of the C# programming language, from source code compilation to its execution within the .NET ecosystem.

docs.title: 1. Source Code and Compilation
docs.text: C# is a high-level, strongly typed language. Unlike languages like C++, C# does not compile directly into machine-specific binary. Instead, when you build a C# project, the C# compiler (Roslyn) translates the source code into Common Intermediate Language (CIL), also known as Microsoft Intermediate Language (MSIL).

docs.title: 2. Assemblies and Metadata
docs.text: The result of the compilation process is an assembly, typically appearing as a .dll or .exe file. These assemblies contain: - CIL Instructions: Platform-independent code that describes the logic of the application. - Metadata: A comprehensive manifest describing every type, method, and attribute defined in the code, as well as the external dependencies required.

docs.title: 3. The Common Language Infrastructure (CLI)
docs.text: C# follows the CLI specification, which is an open standard describing the executable code and runtime environment. This allows C# to be cross-platform and inter-operable with other .NET languages (like F# or VB.NET), as they all compile down to the same intermediate language.

docs.title: 4. The Common Language Runtime (CLR)
docs.text: The CLR is the "engine" that runs C# applications. When a program starts, the CLR handles the execution process. Its primary responsibilities include: - Loading the code: Locating and loading assemblies into memory. - Security: Verifying that the code is type-safe and follows security protocols. - Memory Management: Handling the allocation and deallocation of memory. - Thread Management: Managing parallel execution and CPU utilization.

docs.title: 5. Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation
docs.text: Since CIL is platform-independent, it cannot be executed directly by the CPU. The CLR includes a JIT compiler that converts CIL into native machine code (binary) specifically optimized for the host operating system and processor architecture (e.g., x64, ARM64). This compilation happens at runtime, only when a method is called for the first time.

docs.title: 6. Automatic Memory Management (Garbage Collection)
docs.text: C# manages memory automatically through the Garbage Collector (GC). The GC tracks objects allocated on the "managed heap." When it detects that an object is no longer reachable by the application, it automatically reclaims that memory. This removes the need for manual memory management (like malloc or free in C), significantly reducing memory leaks and pointer-related bugs.

docs.title: 7. The Execution Flow Summary
docs.text:
1. Writing Code: Developer writes C# files (.cs).
  2. Compilation: Roslyn compiles code into an assembly containing CIL and Metadata.
  3. Execution: The user runs the application; the OS starts the .NET Runtime (CLR).
  4. JITing: The CLR’s JIT compiler converts the CIL into native machine instructions.
  5. Runtime Services: The CLR manages execution, providing garbage collection and type safety until the process terminates.
